coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe for visual fluorescence detection of pathogenic bacteria and excessive antibiotics in food safety
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jun
Zhang, Youshu
Li, Yongxin
Zhao, Dan
Zhang, Lina
Bi, Ning
Gou, Jian
Zhao, Tongqian
Jia, Lei
coredata.dc:description: With the rapid development of animal husbandry, animal food safety issues are increasingly exposed. Detecting pathogenic bacteria and residual antibiotics in animal food is of great significance for the early detection and regulation of harmful substances that threaten food safety. However, developing a fluorescent sensor that can detect both pathogenic bacteria and antibiotics is challenging. In this study, an interesting dual-channel ratiometric nanoprobe (CDs-Eu) based on europium-functionalized carbon dots was developed to realize the visual detection of Bacillus anthracis marker (DPA) and tetracycline antibiotics (TC) in a water environment under different excitation channels. This method had high sensitivity, comprehensive response range, anti-interference ability, and visualization for detecting DPA and TC. Combined with intelligent phone color scanning applications, a portable agarose gel nanosensor was designed to achieve multicolor on-site assessment of DPA and TC. In addition, an advanced three-to-six logic gate analysis method was also developed, which was expected to achieve intelligent sensing of DPA and TC in food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacillus anthracis marker
Tetracycline
Multicolor fluorescence sensing
Fluorescence detection platform
Intelligent real-time detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of agarose fluorescence sensing platform and intelligent visual detection of DPA and TC
Detection of DPA and TC in actual samples
Multicolor fluorescence sensing mechanism of DPA and TC
Optimization of DPA and TC detection conditions by CDs-Eu fluorescent nanoprobes
Detection of DPA and TC by CDs-Eu fluorescent nanoprobe
Interference and selective detection of DPA and TC by CDs-Eu fluorescent nanoprobes
Intelligent visual detection of DPA and TC by agarose fluorescence sensing platform
Rapid detection of DPA and TC in actual samples
Application of fluorescent nanoprobe in logic gate circuit

Science and Technology Innovation Team in Henan Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1904168 , No. 51773052 and No. 21805071 ), the foundation of the Science and Technology Innovation Team in Henan Universities ( 24IRTSTHN014 ), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province ( 222300420047 ), and the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University ( T2021-2 ), the projects selected for “Double First Class” creation of safety disciplines at Henan Polytechnic University ( AQ20230760 , AQ20230761 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (a) and the final nanoprobe (b), EDS spectra of CDs (c) and the final nanoprobe (d).
(a) XPS full scan spectrum of CDs-Eu, (b) C1s high-resolution XPS, (c) N1s high-resolution XPS, and (d) O1s high-resolution XPS.

(a) Fluorescence discoloration diagram, (b) fluorescence emission of CDs-Eu with or without DPA (25 μM), (c) fluorescence emission of CDs-Eu with or without TC (15 μM) and (d) diagram of the energy transfer mechanism of CDs-Eu.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-Eu for different DPA concentrations, (b) linear relationship of signal intensity ratio (I616/I420) versus DPA concentrations, (c) the CIE chromaticity to different DPA concentrations (0–70 μM), and (d) probe-sensing color changes at different DPA concentrations.

(a) The luminescence monitoring of CDs-Eu for different TC concentrations, (b) linear relationship between signal intensity ratio (I616/I460) and TC concentrations, (c) the CIE chromaticity to different TC concentrations (0–25 μM), and (d) the probe-sensing color changes at different TC concentrations.
(a) The effect on DPA detection performance (I616/I420) in the presence of common interfering species, (b) Photographs of interferents (fixed at 25 μM) to CDs-Eu under 254 nm UV lamp.

(a) The specific detection of TC by CDs-Eu detection system in the presence of interference source, (b) the response (I616/I460) of CDs-Eu to different antibiotics, and (c) the fluorescence photo of interference substances on CDs-Eu (irradiated at 365 nm).

(a) Color responses of agarose-based nanoprobes to various amounts of DPA, (b) color responses of agarose-based nanoprobes to various amounts of tetracycline, (c) DPA concentration versus agarose color change (R/B) Fitted curve, (d) Fitted curve of TC concentration versus agarose color change (R/G).

(a) Schematic diagram of logic gate circuit diagram, (b) histogram of the relative fluorescence intensity under different fluorescence colors after adding a certain amount of DPA (0–30 μM), (c) histogram of the relative fluorescence intensity under different fluorescence colors after adding a certain amount of TC (0–15 μM), (d) truth table of CDs-Eu logistic transformation.
Schematic diagram of preparing CDs-Eu fluorescent nanoprobe and detecting DPA and TC.
Detection of DPA in actual samples.
Detection of TC in actual samples.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe for visual fluorescence detection of pathogenic bacteria and excessive antibiotics in food safety

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China;

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Resources & Environment, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Resources & Environment, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China;

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rapid development of animal husbandry, animal food safety issues are increasingly exposed. Detecting pathogenic bacteria and residual antibiotics in animal food is of great significance for the early detection and regulation of harmful substances that threaten food safety. However, developing a fluorescent sensor that can detect both pathogenic bacteria and antibiotics is challenging. In this study, an interesting dual-channel ratiometric nanoprobe (CDs-Eu) based on europium-functionalized carbon dots was developed to realize the visual detection of Bacillus anthracis marker (DPA) and tetracycline antibiotics (TC) in a water environment under different excitation channels. This method had high sensitivity, comprehensive response range, anti-interference ability, and visualization for detecting DPA and TC. Combined with intelligent phone color scanning applications, a portable agarose gel nanosensor was designed to achieve multicolor on-site assessment of DPA and TC. In addition, an advanced three-to-six logic gate analysis method was also developed, which was expected to achieve intelligent sensing of DPA and TC in food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The safety problems of animal food are gradually exposed with the fast development of animal husbandry. The pollution of pathogens and the abuse of antibiotics have become essential factors threatening food safety [1,2]. Bacillus anthracis is a pathogen that can infect both animals and humans. After ingestion of more than 104 spores of Bacillus anthracis, it may be fatal without effective drug treatment within 24–28 h [3,4]. The characteristics of high infection and high mortality make the spores of Bacillus anthracis used as biological warfare agents [5–7]. Therefore, detecting Bacillus anthracis is of great interest for the clinical diagnosis of anthrax and the prevention of bioterrorism threats. The main component of bacterial spores is a 2,6-dipyridine carboxylic acid (DPA), which accounts for 5–15 % of the dry weight of the spores. Therefore, DPA can be used as a biomarker of bacterial spores [8,9].

Tetracycline antibiotics are inexpensive and effective antibiotics that have the role of growth hormone and are often used in animal husbandry and aquaculture [10–12]. However, the misuse of tetracycline antibiotics has resulted in their accumulation in the human environment and food [13]. These residual antibiotics will not only enhance the resistance of bacteria in the surrounding environment [14], thus promoting the emergence of superbugs, but also accumulate in the body, posing a significant risk to human health [15–18].

The development of rapid and sensitive detection technology is of great significance for the early detection and control of pathogenic bacteria and antibiotics in animal husbandry development and food safety monitoring. DPA can be detected by some methods, including surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [19], polymerase chain reaction [20], high-performance liquid chromatography [21], immunoassay [22], and molecularly imprinted polymer [23]. To date, numerous analysis methods have been developed for TCs detection, such as chemiluminescence (CE) [24], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [25], electrochemistry [26], colorimetry [27] and mass spectrometry (MS) [28]. However, these detection methods, such as expensive reagents and equipment, complicated steps, and inconvenient operation, often need improvements. Fluorescence analysis has attracted much attention in detecting pathogenic bacteria [29,30] or antibiotics [12,31,32] due to its low detection cost, fast response, and strong specificity recognition ability.

Carbon dots (CDs) have recently become an essential fluorescent probe due to their simple synthesis method, good biocompatibility, anti-photobleaching solid ability, and easy functionalization [33–35]. However, the fluorescence emission intensity and visualization of CDs are relatively low, which limits their application in biological analysis. Rare earth complexes have some outstanding advantages, such as narrow emission peaks, significant Stokes shift, and long fluorescence lifetime, but the toxicity of rare earth metals also limits their biological application. Moreover, the analysis by the change of single wavelength fluorescence intensity is susceptible to excitation wavelength, environmental, and instrument factors, seriously affecting the detection results. Combining rare earth ions with carbon dots to achieve complementary advantages is vital to expand its application in biological analysis. The ratio of the analysis and reference signals measured by a ratiometric fluorescence sensor can significantly improve detection reliability. The preparation of rare earth-doped carbon dots and their fluorescence emission behaviors have attracted much attention [36]. However, most fluorescent probes can only respond to one substance, and developing a fluorescent sensor that can detect pathogenic bacteria and antibiotics is challenging.

Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence probe composed of rare earth ions doped carbon dots (CDs-Eu) was successfully fabricated to detect DPA and TC (Scheme 1 ). The amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of blue-emitting CDs provide coordination sites for Eu3+ ions. However, the coordination number of Eu3+ ions is unsaturated, and a large number of coordinated water leads to fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence of red-emitting Eu3+ could be restored by adding DPA or TC at a distinguished excitation wavelength. The fluorescence response of this dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe is quite sensitive and selective to DPA and TC through different channels. This dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence probe can be applied for the quantitative detection of DPA and TC through multicolor changes and realize rapid semi-quantitative on-site sensing of DPA and TC using smartphone color scanning APP by constructing agarose gel sensors. This research can offer a new idea for designing and preparing multifunctional probes and has potential application prospects in animal husbandry development and food safety.

Polyetherimide (PEI, MW 10000), Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O), tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris) and 2,6-Dicarboxylic Pyridine Acid (DPA) were purchased from China Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Salicylic acid was purchased from Yantai Shuangshuang Chemical Co., China. Tetracycline (TC) and other reagents were obtained from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd., China.

The microstructure of the samples was performed by Tecnai-G2-F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM, 300 kV accelerated voltage) with an energy spectrometer. The powder X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) was obtained using the Rigaku Dmax2400 diffractometer of CuKα radiation in the 2θ range of 10–70° with a scan rate of 10°/min. In the range of 4000–400 cm−1, the material was characterized by Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy using standard KBr disk technology and Bruker V70 FTIR spectrometer. The corresponding ultraviolet absorption spectra were recorded by PerkinElmerlda 950 spectrophotometer. Elemental compositions of samples were analyzed by Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The fluorescence characteristics of samples were determined using a Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer.

CDs were prepared by hydrothermal carbonization. Briefly, salicylic acid (1.0 g) and PEI (0.1 g) were mixed in 60 mL of pure water and reacted in Teflon stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 8 h. The obtained brown solution was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 1 min, and the resulting solution was transferred to the cellulose membrane (MWCO 1000) and purified with ultrapure water for 24 h. Finally, CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

Firstly, CDs (100 mg) and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (50 mg) were mixed in ethanol (30 mL) and then stirred at 60 °C for 10 h. The solution was filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane and then transferred to cellulose membrane (MWCO 1000) for ultrapure water dialysis for 12 h to obtain the final CDs-Eu product.

DPA assessment: The probe (200 μg) was dispersed in 2 mL of a buffer solution with a pH of 7, and the change of fluorescence intensities of the probe with different amounts of DPA were investigated (excitation wavelength was 260 nm). DPA sensing and anti-interference test methods in actual samples were the same as above.

TC assessment: The probe (300 μg) was dispersed in 2 mL of a buffer solution with a pH of 9, and the change of fluorescence intensities of the probe with different amounts of TC were investigated (excitation wavelength was 365 nm). TC sensing and anti-interference test methods in actual samples were the same as above.

The limit of detection (LOD) can be calculated by D = 3σ/k equation, where σ is the relative standard deviation of the blank sample and k is the slope of the calibration line [15,31]. The σ value can be calculated by multiple sets of blank experiments. The K value is obtained by linear fitting. With the increasing of DPA concentrations, the fluorescent intensity ratio of the peak at 616 and 420 nm showed linear variation, and k is the slope of the calibration line. Similar to the above method, with the increase of TC concentration, the fluorescence intensity ratio at 616 nm and 460 nm changes linearly, and k is the slope of the calibration curve.

In order to further evaluate the anti-interference and selectivity of probes for DPA and TC detection, a series of common metal ions, representative organic small molecules, and different aqueous solutions such as antibiotics were configured. In the anti-interference experiment of DPA and TC detection, 10 μM and 15 μM interference substances were added, respectively. A similar method in Section 2.5 was used to replace DPA and TC. At the same time, the fluorescence intensity changes caused by interference substances on the probe detection of target substances in the presence of DPA (25 μM) and TC (15 μM) were further tested.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of agarose fluorescence sensing platform and intelligent visual detection of DPA and TC
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of agarose fluorescence sensing system for DPA and TC sensing: 300 mg of agarose in 10 mL Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7) was heated to wholly dissolved. Add 25 mg of CDs-Eu powder to continue heating and stirring for 30 min. Then, the solution was taken out and poured into the template for natural cooling to obtain an agarose fluorescence-detecting platform for DPA assessment. The preparation of an agarose fluorescence sensing platform for TC detection was similar to that of DPA, while Tris-HCl buffer was replaced with pH 9.

Intelligent visual detection: Under 254 nm and 365 nm UV lights, a series of concentrations of DPA and TC were dropped on the prepared agarose sensing platforms, respectively, and the photo of the fluorescence color response of the sensing platform to different concentrations of analytes was taken by mobile phone. The color scanner App can accurately obtain the corresponding relationship between DPA and TC at different concentrations and RGB values of fluorescence color. With the concentration of DPA and TC as the abscissa and the chromaticity ratios of R/B (red/blue) and R/G (red/green) as the ordinate, the relationship between the concentration of target detection and the chromaticity ratio was calculated by linear fitting. In the actual detection, the qualitative and semi-quantitative detection of DPA and TC was realized by testing the ratio of R/B and R/G in the fluorescent color of agarose sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA and TC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The water sample was first filtered by 0.22 μm membrane to remove the insoluble matter, and the experiment was carried out with DPA. The soil sample was dispersed in 10 mL of tap water and filtered by the same membrane for DPA detection. The 200 μg CDs-Eu was dispersed in 2 mL of pure water and added with an unknown sample concentration to be tested. The fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite sensing material was investigated under 260 nm excitation. The concentration of DPA in the sample to be tested was calculated by fitting the standard curve.

For TC assessment, honey and milk purchased locally were pretreated according to the method in the literature [37]. Protein and fat in the samples were removed first, and then the TC standard solutions were prepared using the liquid samples. Under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded, and then the concentration of TC in the sample to be tested was calculated by fitting the standard curve.

The morphology of carbon dots before and after europium functionalized modification was studied by transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 1a, the size of carbon dots was about 2∼4 nm, and they were uniformly distributed. After functional modification of europium ions (Fig. 1b), CDs nanoparticles aggregated due to the formation of CDs-Eu complexes between Eu3+ ions and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs [38,39]. It can also be speculated from the EDS spectrum (Fig. 1c and d) that europium was successfully modified on the surface of the carbon dots.

It can be seen from the XRD pattern (Fig. S2a) that the peak shape and peak position of the samples were unchanged before and after the modification of Eu3+, indicating that the disordered structure of carbon dots was maintained in the presence of Eu3+. The characteristic functional groups around CDs and CDs-Eu can be identified by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S2b). The broad absorption band at 3376 cm−1 of CDs and CDs-Eu can be attributed to the N–H stretching vibration of the –NH2 group and the peaks at 2950 and 2849 cm−1 were the characteristic peaks of the C–H stretching vibration of the –CH2 [39,40]. The characteristic peaks of CDs at 1655, 1603, 1460, and 1030 cm−1 were vibrational peaks for CO, N–H, C–H, and C–O, respectively [38,40], with the addition of Eu3+, the absorption peak at 1655 cm−1 redshifted to 1610 cm−1, which can be due to the chelation of Eu3+ and carboxyl groups changing the symmetric characteristic vibration. XPS full-scan spectrum in Fig. 2 a indicated that CDs-Eu contained prominent C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Eu 3d peaks. In the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b), the peaks at 284.9 eV, 286.2 eV, and 289.8 eV corresponded to C–C/C–H, C–N/C–O, and CO, respectively [41,42]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) had three peaks at 399.8, 401.9, and 407.1 eV, which can be attributed to N–H, C–N, and –NO3, respectively [43,44]. Two peaks at 531.8 and 532.8 eV can be analyzed from the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) of CDs-Eu, which can be attributed to CO and C–OH, respectively [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor fluorescence sensing mechanism of DPA and TC
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were a new class of non-toxic nano-fluorescent materials that could show photoluminescence related to the excitation wavelength. CDs can also be used as the internal fluorescent standards in ratiometric fluorescent probes. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescent nanoprobe emits dark blue (420 nm) or blue-green fluorescence (460 nm) under the excitation of 260 nm or 365 nm in the absence of both DPA and TC. After adding DPA to the detection system, the red fluorescence channel of Eu3+ was activated at 260 nm, and the fluorescence color gradually changed from dark blue to red. This phenomenon also occurred in the presence of TC, but it can only be detected at the excitation of 365 nm, and the fluorescence color changed from green to red. As shown in Fig. 3b and c, the transition of Eu3+ from 5D0 to 7FJ (J = 1, 2, 3, 4) led to a remarkable enhancement of the emission peaks at 593, 616, 652, and 695 nm after the addition of 25 μM DPA or 15 μM TC, where the transition of 5D0-7F2 was the most prominent. The enhancement of Eu3+ characteristic peaks was related to the following two factors. On the one hand, DPA or TC could coordinate with Eu3+ and enhance the fluorescence intensity through the antenna effect (AE), thereby triggering the ultra-sensitive energy transition at 616 nm [46–48]. More precisely, the lowest triplet energy levels of DPA and TC can match well with the energy levels of Eu3+, prompting them to produce adequate energy transfer to Eu3+ (Fig. 3d) [49,50]. On the other hand, the coordination groups of DPA and TC, such as –CO or –COOH, can replace the coordinated H2O molecules bound to Eu, inhibiting the non-radiative quenching effect [42,50]. This was further demonstrated by the variation in the fluorescence lifetime of the nanoprobe after coordination with DPA and TC. Fig. S3 also indicated that the addition of DPA or TC significantly enhanced the fluorescence lifetime of CDs-Eu [51,52]. Therefore, the probe can detect DPA and TC at different excitation wavelengths.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of DPA and TC detection conditions by CDs-Eu fluorescent nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation wavelength and pH value were optimized to determine optimal DPA and TC detection conditions. Fig. S4 (a) indicates the optimal excitation wavelength can be determined by analyzing the fluorescence ratio (I616/I420 or I616/I460) under different excitations with a certain concentration of DPA or TC. The above analysis shows the optimal conditions for detecting DPA (260 nm excited, pH = 7) and TC (365 nm excited, pH = 9).

In addition, although temperature and incubation time also have a slight effect on the sensing of the probe, this effect is relatively small from our previous work. For example, the ambient temperature may affect the binding of DPA and TC with rare-earth Eu ions, but common assays are usually performed at room temperature, and this lower temperature variation interval does not have much effect on the generation of complexes. Furthermore, the results of our group's previous studies show that the incubation time for the detection of DPA and TC is generally below 2 min, so in order to ensure the sufficient reaction between the test material and the rare earth, we chose an incubation time of 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA and TC by CDs-Eu fluorescent nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions (Ex = 260 nm, pH = 7), the probe's response to DPA was detected by fluorescence titration. Fig. 4 a illustrated that the intensity at 420 nm decreased slightly with the increase of DPA concentrations from 0 to 70 μM. In comparison, the intensity at 616 nm gradually increased and reached a maximum of 70 μM. Fig. 4b showed a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I616/I420) and the concentration of DPA, which can be expressed as Y1 = 0.14235 + 0.02224X1 (R2 = 0.95054) and Y2 = 0.063X2-0.20159, (R2 = 0.99919) between 0-10 μM and 10–70 μM, respectively. According to the calculation formula: limit of detection (LOD) = 3σ/slope (σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample), the LOD value was determined to be 10.5 nM. The CIE coordinates (Table S1) were calculated based on the emission spectra of DPA titration. The CIE coordinates to different DPA concentrations (0–70 μM) shown in Fig. 4c gradually changed from blue (0.2223, 0.1606) to red (0.4265, 0.2646). More interesting, the CDs-Eu nanoprobe can achieve visualized sensing of DPA under 254 nm ultraviolet light. Fig. 4d indicated that the fluorescence color gradually changed from dark blue to red as the DPA concentration increased from 0 μM to 15 μM.

Similarly, the detection effect of CDs-Eu on TC was evaluated under the optimal TC detection conditions (Ex = 365 nm, pH = 9). With the increasing of TC concentrations (0–25 μM), the emission intensity at 460 nm slightly decreased. In comparison, the intensity at 616 nm gradually increased to the maximum at 25 μM (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b shows an excellent linear correlation, and from which the LOD and LOQ were determined to be 5.42 nM and 16.42 nM [53]. More importantly, the CIE coordinates (Table S2) gradually shifted from (0.1924, 0.2228) to (0.3607, 0.2716), and the fluorescence color of the sample gradually changed from blue-green to red, which the naked eye can directly observe (Fig. 5c and d).

To further evaluate the performance of this probe for the detection of TC and DPA, we compared the performance advantages of this probe with those already reported in terms of Linear range and LOD. The results in Tables S3 and S4 showed that the probe has high sensitivity, a wide linear range, and a simple detection process, which can meet the requirements of food quality and safety control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference and selective detection of DPA and TC by CDs-Eu fluorescent nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: The possible interference of various ions in the water environment to the probe was simulated. The effects of common cations, some small molecular such as benzoic acid (BA), isonicotinic acid (INA) et al. on the performance of DPA detection were studied. Fig. 6 a and b indicated these interfering substances had little interference with the DPA detection sensing system, and the CDs-Eu fluorescent nanoprobe had good selectivity for DPA.

The same method was used to study the interference of various small molecules, including L-aspartic acid (Asp), 2,6-pyridine dicarboxylic acid (DPA), Histidine (His), lysine (Lys), glucose (Glu), L-cysteine (Cys), L-homocysteine (Hcy), glutathione (GSH), L-proline (Pro) and common ions in the virtual environment on the TC detection sensing system. Fig. 7 a and c proved that the presence of these small molecules or ion interferents did not affect the TC sensing effect of the composite probe. Furthermore, CDs-Eu had better responsiveness and specificity to TC (Fig. 7b), which indicated excellent sample analysis performance under complex conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intelligent visual detection of DPA and TC by agarose fluorescence sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: A multicolor intelligent sensing platform based on agarose was designed to develop the practical application of the probe further. Compared with the paper-based sensing platform introduced in some literature [9,54], it had the advantages of no background interference, more sensitivity to color changes, and being more intuitive. As shown in Fig. 8 a and b, the color intelligent sensor platform changed from blue or blue-green to red under the 254 or 365 nm UV lamp to detect DPA or TC. Fig. 8c and d showed a linear relationship between Red/Blue values (R/B) and concentration after scanning by the color scanning APP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid detection of DPA and TC in actual samples
Application of fluorescent nanoprobe in logic gate circuit
ce:section.ce:para: Since the CDs-Eu fluorescent nanosensor had a good fluorescence signal response to DPA and TC, a three-input six-output logic circuit can be designed to perform semi-quantitative detection of DPA and TC (Fig. 9 a). As shown in Fig. 9b, the relative intensity of the blue fluorescence emission at 420 nm (I-blue) and the relative intensity of the red fluorescence emission at 616 nm (I-red) were defined as the input signals 1 and 2 of the DPA sensing, respectively. The relative intensity of the green fluorescence emission at 460 nm (I-green) and the red fluorescence emission at 616 nm (I-red) in Fig. 9c corresponded to the double input signals 3 and 2 of TC detection, respectively. In detecting DPA and TC, when the blue, red, and green fluorescence signals were detected at inputs 1, 2, and 3 of the system, the input signal was defined as 1; otherwise, it was 0.

According to the data after normalized luminescence intensity, 5 μM and 20 μM were used as thresholds for DPA detection. When the concentration of DPA was below 5 μM, the system was defined as emitting only blue fluorescence; the input 1-terminal signal was 1, and the input 2-terminal signal was 0. When the amount of DPA was between 5 and 20 μM, the system was defined as the blue and red fluorescence was simultaneously excited; that was, the input end 1, 2, and the end signal was 1. When the amount of DPA was more than 20 μM, only red light was excited, and the signs at the input 1 and 2 ends were 0 and 1, respectively. Fig. 9d shows the truth table for detecting DPA. When the input is (1, 0), and the output was (1, 0, 0), the amount of DPA was below 5 μM. Inputs (1, 1) and outputs (0, 1, 0) indicated that the amount of DPA was 5–20 μM. Input (0, 1) and output (0, 0, 1) indicated that the amount of DPA was more significant than 20 μM.

When detecting TC, 2 μM and 6.5 μM were used as thresholds. Similar to the principle of detecting DPA, the system was defined as only emitting green light when the concentration of TC was below 2 μM. When TC concentration was between 2 and 6.5 μM, the system emitted green and red fluorescence. In contrast, the system emitted only red fluorescence with the TC dosage more incredible than 6.5 μM. The accurate value table in Fig. 9d shows input (0, 1) and output (1, 0, 0), indicating that the TC concentration was less than 2 μM. Input (1, 1), output (0, 1, 0), input (1, 0), output (0, 0, 1), indicating that the TC concentration was more than 6.5 μM.

In this work, a novel strategy for detecting TC and DPA based on europium-functionalized carbon dots was developed. Based on the dual channel characteristics, the probe can detect DPA and TC, which was rarely reported in other studies. It can not only achieve ultra-sensitive (LOD:10.5 nM) and wide-range detection (50 nM-70 μM) of DPA, but also had an excellent fluorescence response to TC in the 50 nM-25 μM, and LOD for TC was 5.42 nM. In addition, combined with the advantages of the probe, an intelligent visual fluorescence sensing platform was constructed with agarose gel to achieve the visual detecting of DPA and TC. At the same time, the fast intelligent analysis performance of the probe for DPA and TC is improved by using the chromaticity calculation APP in the smartphone. To further study the application of the probe in the virtual environment, the response of DPA and TC in actual samples was further simulated here. The experimental data showed that the probe also had an excellent detection effect in actual samples. Since the presence of DPA and TC will cause the change of fluorescence color in the detection system, a logic gate relationship switch for detection of DPA and TC was designed and studied, which was expected to achieve intelligent sensing of various toxic substances in the future.

Jun Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Youshu Zhang: Visualization, Investigation, Software. Yongxin Li: Formal analysis, Software. Dan Zhao: Software, Validation. Lina Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Ning Bi: Software, Validation. Jian Gou: Formal analysis, Software, Methodology. Tongqian Zhao: Conceptualization, Investigation. Lei Jia: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1904168, No. 51773052 and No. 21805071), the foundation of the Science and Technology Innovation Team in Henan Universities (24IRTSTHN014), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province (222300420047), and the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University (T2021-2), the projects selected for “Double First Class” creation of safety disciplines at Henan Polytechnic University (AQ20230760, AQ20230761).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2023.120303.
